statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, print the top view of the binary tree. The top view of a binary tree is the set of nodes you will see when the tree is viewed from the top. For this problem, we will consider the leftmost node at each level as the top view node. The tree nodes are numbered from 0 to n-1, where n is the number of nodes in the tree, and the nodes are numbered in the order they appear in the pre-order traversal of the tree. The root of the tree is node 0. The tree nodes have unique values. The input to the problem will be the pre-order traversal of the tree and the corresponding horizontal distance of each node from the root node. The output should be the node values in the top view of the tree, from left to right.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of nodes in the tree. The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the pre-order traversal of the tree. The third line contains n space-separated integers representing the horizontal distance of each node from the root node.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a single line containing the node values in the top view of the tree, from left to right, separated by spaces.
statement.notes: The input tree is a binary tree where each node has a unique value and the root node is node 0. The tree nodes are numbered from 0 to n-1, where n is the number of nodes in the tree, and the nodes are numbered in the order they appear in the pre-order traversal of the tree.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree
The values of the nodes in the tree
The horizontal distances of the nodes from the root node

examples.explanation: The top view of the binary tree is 4, 2, 1, 3, 6
The top view of the binary tree is 1, 3

hints.text: The problem requires finding the leftmost node at each level of a binary tree when viewed from the top. Consider how you can use a data structure to keep track of nodes at each level.

The input is given in pre-order traversal along with the horizontal distance of each node from the root. Think about how you can utilize the horizontal distance to identify the leftmost node at each level.

You will likely need to use a data structure that maps horizontal distances to node values. Consider how you can update this data structure as you process each node in the pre-order traversal to ensure you capture the leftmost node at each level.

editorial.title: Optimal Approach for Top View of Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The key observation here is that we need to maintain a mapping of horizontal distances to the topmost node at that distance. This can be achieved by using a hashmap to store the nodes at each horizontal distance as we traverse the tree.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve traversing the tree for each node and calculating its horizontal distance from the root. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2) due to the repeated traversals, making it inefficient for large trees.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using a level order traversal (BFS) and maintaining a hashmap to store the topmost node at each horizontal distance. This allows us to visit each node exactly once, achieving a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided solution uses a deque to perform a level order traversal of the tree, storing the topmost node at each horizontal distance in a hashmap. It then constructs the result by iterating over the range of horizontal distances and retrieving the corresponding node values.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that it visits each node exactly once and maintains the topmost node at each horizontal distance. The use of a hashmap ensures that we can efficiently retrieve the topmost node at each distance, resulting in a correct top view of the binary tree.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution with a sample input reveals that it correctly handles edge cases such as an empty tree and a tree with a single node. It also correctly calculates the top view for trees with multiple nodes and varying horizontal distances.
commonMistakes: Not using a hashmap to store the topmost node at each horizontal distance, resulting in a brute force approach with O(n^2) time complexity.
Not maintaining a correct mapping of horizontal distances to node values, leading to incorrect results.
Not handling edge cases such as an empty tree or a tree with a single node.
Using a recursive approach instead of an iterative one, which can lead to stack overflow for large trees.
Not considering the horizontal distance when traversing the tree, resulting in an incorrect top view.
